_comment: Adversarial divergence scenarios for the events.sql per-field merge vs detect_merge (E05-T2.6, generated by the conflict-merge-divergence-hunt workflow). Each is replayed through the real RPC on EphemeralPostgres and asserted equal to detect_merge (test_conflict_rpc_parity.py). 'base' is a 1-based index into 'prefix' (0 or null = genesis). Payloads use only codec-valid scalars (strings/ints/bools/null) + nested int containers; floats are intentionally absent (the signing codec refuses them, so no committed event can carry one — the 'numeric 1.0 vs 1' divergence the hunt posited is unreachable).

scenarios.name: numeric_scale_1.0_vs_1_field_head
scenarios.rationale: Field-head for story_points is rev1 carrying JSON 1.0 (in window since base=1, head=2, 1<rev<=2 fails for rev1... wait base=1 excludes rev1). Set base=null on incoming instead via a separate vector; here incoming.base=1 so window is (1,2]=only rev2. So contender for story_points is None -> both APPLY, no conflict. The DIVERGENCE target: when base brings rev1 into window the value 1.0 (stored as json string '1.0', cast to jsonb numeric 1.0) is compared to incoming int 1. jsonb IS DISTINCT FROM treats 1.0 and 1 as EQUAL (numeric), so plpgsql emits NO conflict; a naive Python canonicalize comparing float/Decimal 1.0 to int 1 or the literal token may flag a CONFLICT. This is the core json-vs-jsonb numeric-equality split.

scenarios.name: numeric_scale_in_window_genesis_base
scenarios.rationale: Genesis-base incoming (base=null->0), head=1, window=(0,1]=rev1. Field-head of story_points is rev1 with json 2.0. plpgsql casts payload->'story_points' to jsonb numeric 2.0 and compares IS DISTINCT FROM incoming jsonb 2 -> NOT distinct -> NO conflict. Python canonicalize(2.0) vs canonicalize(2): if canonicalize does not numerically normalize trailing-zero floats to ints it returns CONFLICT. Pure numeric-equality divergence with the field-head guaranteed in-window (unlike the prior vector). title is untouched by incoming so no decision for it.

scenarios.name: field_set_before_and_within_window_head_is_inwindow_setter
scenarios.rationale: assignee is set BEFORE the window (rev1='alice', base=1 excludes it) and WITHIN the window (rev3='bob'). Window=(1,3]. Field-head MUST be the in-window setter rev3='bob', NOT the pre-window rev1='alice'. Both impls should pick rev3 via order-by-rev-desc / latest-in-window. Tests parity of the 'field-head is the in-window setter, not an earlier out-of-window writer' rule with an interleaved unrelated write (rev2) between the two assignee writes. Predicted: assignee conflicts against 'bob' (contending_revision=3, head_value='bob').

scenarios.name: multi_field_conflict_array_ordering_jsonb_keysort
scenarios.rationale: Window=(1,2]=rev2 which is field-head for all of z,a,mm. All three differ -> all three CONFLICT in both impls (set membership identical). The DIVERGENCE is ARRAY ORDER: plpgsql iterates jsonb_each(e->'payload') which yields keys in Postgres jsonb storage order (shortest-key-first then bytewise: 'a','z','mm'), while Python iterates incoming.payload.items() in insertion order ('z','a','mm'). If detect_merge parity compares the conflicts[] array positionally rather than as a set, the orderings diverge. Key lengths (1,1,2) force a non-lexicographic jsonb order distinct from insertion order.

scenarios.name: field_head_shadowed_by_later_tombstone_carrying_same_field
scenarios.rationale: Window=(1,4]. A tombstone sits at rev3 INSIDE the window carrying label='blue' in its payload. Python edit-vs-delete: tombstone_rev=max(window tombstone)=3, base(1)<3 -> returns the SINGLE __entity__ conflict (contending_revision=3, head_value=None, incoming_value=whole payload). plpgsql: v_tomb_rev=max(rev,op='tombstone',1<rev<=4)=3 (not null) -> emits the __entity__ conflict and SKIPS per-field. Both should agree on __entity__. BUT this stresses: (a) tombstone op-filtering in field-head selection is moot because edit-vs-delete short-circuits first; (b) the tombstone's payload label='blue' must NOT be treated as a field-head. If either impl forgets the edit-vs-delete short-circuit and falls to per-field, plpgsql's op<>'tombstone' filter would pick rev2='green' as label head while a buggy path could pick the tombstone's 'blue'. Predicted conflict field is __entity__ only.

scenarios.name: head_value_set_incoming_explicit_null_sqlnull_vs_jsonnull
scenarios.rationale: Window=(1,2]=rev2, field-head assignee='bob' (json string). Incoming assignee is explicit JSON null. plpgsql: v_in_value from jsonb_each(incoming payload) is jsonb 'null' (a jsonb scalar, NOT sql NULL); v_head_value=(payload::jsonb)->'assignee'= jsonb '"bob"'. jsonb '"bob"' IS DISTINCT FROM jsonb 'null' -> TRUE -> CONFLICT with head_value='bob', incoming_value=null. Python: canonicalize('bob') != canonicalize(None) -> CONFLICT. Likely aligned IF plpgsql correctly carries jsonb null (not SQL NULL). The divergence risk is that '->' / jsonb_each null handling or an IS DISTINCT FROM with an accidental sql-NULL coercion flips the result, and that incoming_value serialization (json null vs absent) differs in the emitted record. Mirror/inverse of golden S2.

scenarios.name: numeric-norm-float-vs-int-idempotent-vs-conflict
scenarios.rationale: Field-head value is the float 1.0; incoming is the int 1. Python compares via canonicalize(json.dumps): "1.0" != "1" so it emits a conflict on `priority`. plpgsql casts stored json to jsonb and compares jsonb IS DISTINCT FROM jsonb; Postgres numeric normalizes 1.0 == 1, so it emits NO conflict (treats it idempotent). This is the cleanest json-vs-jsonb numeric-equality divergence and is not covered by R2 (same-value) / R3 (different-value) because those use values that agree under BOTH equality notions.

scenarios.name: numeric-norm-via-post-tombstone-revival-fieldhead
scenarios.rationale: Tombstone at rev2 is OUTSIDE the window (incoming base=2, window=(2,3]) so neither impl short-circuits to __entity__ edit-vs-delete; the revival patch at rev3 (committed AFTER the tombstone) becomes the field-head in both. The op<>'tombstone' filter is exercised and agrees. The divergence is purely the float-vs-int compare on the revived field: Python flags `points` (3.0 != 3 as strings), plpgsql sees jsonb 3.0 == 3 and flags nothing. Reaches the numeric crack through the tombstone-revival path the lens targets.

scenarios.name: numeric-norm-mixed-real-plus-spurious-field
scenarios.rationale: Multi-field incoming where one field is a genuine string conflict (title A vs B, agreed by both) and one is a numeric-normalization-only difference (estimate 100.0 vs 100). Python emits conflicts on BOTH title and estimate; plpgsql emits only title because jsonb 100.0 == 100. Proves the divergence changes the actual emitted conflicts[] set/length, not just an idempotent-vs-conflict verdict label, so the golden-vector cross-check would show a field-set mismatch.

scenarios.name: numeric-norm-negative-zero
scenarios.rationale: Edge of numeric normalization: head value -0.0, incoming 0. Python canonicalize(json.dumps(-0.0))="-0.0" vs json.dumps(0)="0" -> conflict on `delta`. Postgres jsonb numeric treats -0.0 and 0 as equal -> no conflict. A nastier variant of the float/int crack (signed zero) likely missed by any hand-written vector.

scenarios.name: patch-base-AT-head-tombstone-window-empty
scenarios.rationale: Incoming base=2 equals head_rev=2 so the window (2,2] is empty in both impls: Python's tombstone_rev=None and the field loop sees no contender (apply, zero conflicts); plpgsql's guard v_head > v_base_eff is FALSE so the per-field block is skipped entirely (zero conflicts). Both emit the SAME empty conflict set, so per-field parity holds (low divergence risk) -- but I flag it because it exposes a shared semantic hole vs MOD-26 B5: an edit committed with base AT a committed tombstone produces no edit-vs-delete conflict_record from detect_merge here, so sticky-tombstone routing must be enforced elsewhere (the fold), not by this merge block. Useful as a parity guard / boundary witness rather than a divergence.

scenarios.name: bool_true_vs_int_one_field_head
scenarios.rationale: Field-head in window (B,H] = rev2, which set flag to the JSON integer 1. Incoming sets flag to JSON boolean true. plpgsql compares jsonb 1 IS DISTINCT FROM jsonb true -> the two are different jsonb types -> TRUE -> conflict on flag. Python canonicalize equality almost certainly bottoms out in Python value comparison where 1 == True is True (the int/bool identity quirk), making it IDEMPOTENT -> no conflict. This is the cleanest opposite-direction split under the lens: jsonb says conflict, Python says idempotent. Same trap fires for {flag:0} head vs {flag:false} incoming.

scenarios.name: int_vs_float_same_magnitude
scenarios.rationale: Head (rev2) stored priority as integer 2 in the json column; incoming carries 2.0. After payload::jsonb cast, Postgres normalizes 2.0 -> numeric 2, so jsonb 2 IS DISTINCT FROM jsonb 2 is FALSE -> idempotent, no conflict. Python detect_merge is idempotent only if canonicalize collapses 2 and 2.0 (value-equality: 2 == 2.0 is True). If canonicalize is string/repr-based (json.dumps without numeric normalization -> '2' vs '2.0'), Python flags a conflict while jsonb does not. This sits exactly on the json->jsonb number normalization seam the spec flags as the two-language risk. Predicted set assumes the value-equality reading of canonicalize.

scenarios.name: high_precision_int_beyond_2pow53
scenarios.rationale: Two integers one apart, both above 2^53. jsonb stores them as exact numeric, so 9007199254740992 IS DISTINCT FROM 9007199254740993 is TRUE -> conflict (head_value 9007199254740992). Python is conflict-consistent ONLY if canonicalize preserves integer precision. If canonicalize coerces through float64 (e.g. float(x) or a json round-trip that yields floats), both collapse to 9.007199254740992e15 and become equal -> idempotent -> Python drops the conflict that jsonb keeps. This is the precision-loss mirror of the int/float scenario: divergence direction is jsonb-conflict vs Python-idempotent if any float canonicalization exists.

scenarios.name: nested_object_key_reorder
scenarios.rationale: Field-level test using two scalar keys whose only difference between head (rev2) and incoming is insertion ORDER. Postgres normalizes jsonb object key order, so each scalar field compares equal -> no conflict. Python detect_merge iterates per field, so per-field value-equality also says idempotent for both fields. Consistent under per-field iteration; the divergence only materializes if either side compared whole sub-objects via an unsorted string canonicalize. Included to confirm the per-field loop neutralizes key-order, and as the structural-canonicalize control next to the genuinely nested array probe. Predicted: no conflicts.

scenarios.name: nested_array_numeric_normalization
scenarios.rationale: checklist is a nested ARRAY field value, so the comparison is whole-value, not per-element-decomposed by either side. Head (rev2) stored [1.0,2.0,3.0]; payload::jsonb normalizes the inner numerics to [1,2,3], so jsonb [1,2,3] IS DISTINCT FROM jsonb [1,2,3] is FALSE -> idempotent. Python is idempotent only if canonicalize deep-normalizes numerics inside arrays (value-equality: [1.0,2.0,3.0] == [1,2,3] is True in Python). If canonicalize is a string dump that does not normalize inner number formatting ('[1.0, 2.0, 3.0]' vs '[1, 2, 3]'), Python flags a conflict that jsonb never sees. Directly targets nested-value numeric normalization across the json->jsonb cast.

scenarios.name: field_head_collapse_int_vs_float
scenarios.rationale: Combines multi-write field-head collapse (R4-style) with the numeric-format seam. base=1, head=3, window (1,3] = {rev2(points=8), rev3(points=8.0)}. Both implementations must pick the LATEST contender (rev3, value 8.0) by revision desc, NOT rev2. plpgsql: rev3 8.0 normalizes to jsonb 8, incoming 8 -> not distinct -> idempotent. Python: contender rev3 value 8.0 vs incoming 8 -> idempotent only under value-equality canonicalize. Two ways to diverge: (a) if field-head selection differs (e.g. ordering or the ? operator picks a different contender), the contending_revision/head_value differ; (b) if canonicalize is string-based, Python sees '8.0' vs '8' as a conflict against rev3 while jsonb stays idempotent. Predicted (value-equality reference): no conflict, with the contender pinned to rev3 if one were raised.

scenarios.name: numeric_dot_zero_idempotent_vs_conflict
scenarios.rationale: Committed field-head stores estimate=1.0 (json). Incoming sets estimate=1 base=genesis(0), so it falls in the window and the field-head is the contender. plpgsql casts the stored json 1.0 to jsonb, which normalizes to 1, and the incoming payload->'estimate' is jsonb 1, so `jsonb IS DISTINCT FROM jsonb` is FALSE -> idempotent, NO conflict. Python canonicalize() compares 1.0 vs 1 as distinct Python scalars/representations (float vs int) and flags a CONFLICT. This is the cleanest json-vs-jsonb numeric-normalization divergence.

scenarios.name: numeric_exponent_normalization
scenarios.rationale: Probes a second numeric-equality class: integer head vs float-with-trailing-zero incoming. jsonb normalizes 1000.0 to 1000 so plpgsql sees them equal (idempotent, no conflict). Python's canonicalize sees float 1000.0 vs int 1000 and, depending on whether canonicalize coerces to a common numeric type or compares repr/type, flags a CONFLICT. Same root cause as scenario A but reversed direction (head int, incoming float) to catch an asymmetric canonicalize that only normalizes one side.

scenarios.name: far_back_field_head_numeric
scenarios.rationale: Dual-lens: field-head selection several events back (estimate set only at rev1, then 4 intervening events touch other fields) combined with json-vs-jsonb numeric normalization. Both impls must walk the (0,5] window and pick rev1 as the estimate contender (ORDER BY revision DESC + `? 'estimate'` in plpgsql vs latest-with-field in Python) -> they should AGREE on contender=rev1. They then DIVERGE on the value: jsonb 5 vs 5.0 is equal (plpgsql idempotent, no conflict) but Python int 5 vs float 5.0 may conflict. Confirms field-head selection is correct AND isolates the numeric divergence at a far-back head.

scenarios.name: null_head_contender_beats_earlier_setter
scenarios.rationale: Tests field-head selection when the latest setter writes JSON null (explicit unassign). The field-head in (0,2] is rev2 with assignee=null, NOT rev1 'alice'. plpgsql relies on `(payload::jsonb) ? 'assignee'` which returns TRUE for a key whose value is null, so it must pick rev2 and report head_value=jsonb null; null IS DISTINCT FROM 'bob' -> CONFLICT contending_revision=2, head_value=null. Python picks contender rev2 (field present) and canonicalize(None) vs 'bob' -> CONFLICT. If plpgsql's `?` filter or the IS DISTINCT FROM null handling misbehaves (e.g. treats null head as 'no contender'), it would instead pick rev1 'alice' or apply with no conflict -> divergence in contending_revision/head_value.

scenarios.name: never_set_field_plus_conflicting_field
scenarios.rationale: Per-field loop boundary: incoming sets one field never present in the prefix (priority -> no contender -> apply) and one field that collides (title 'A' vs 'Z' -> conflict). plpgsql's jsonb_each must iterate both keys, find no field-head for priority (`? 'priority'` false -> found=false -> skip) and a field-head rev1 for title. Python: contender None for priority -> apply; contender rev1 for title -> conflict. Both should flag ONLY title. Exposes any divergence where plpgsql mishandles a missing-contender field (e.g. emits a spurious conflict with head_value null) or where the loop short-circuits after the first field. Strings avoid the numeric-normalization confound, isolating the no-contender boundary.

scenarios.name: base_equals_head_same_field_different_value
scenarios.rationale: Boundary probe: incoming base_rev == committed head (=2). The (B,H] window is empty (B==H). plpgsql guards the whole per-field block with `v_head > v_base_eff` (strict), so 2 > 2 is FALSE and it emits no conflicts. Python builds window base<rev<=head = (2,2] = empty, contender None for title -> apply, no conflict. Both must agree on NO conflict (the writer saw the latest title). This pins the empty-window/base==head boundary even though the incoming overwrites the same field with a new value -- a naive off-by-one (using >= instead of >, or base<rev vs base<=rev) on either side would here produce a spurious title conflict on one impl only.
